{{Short description|Shingon Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{About|a Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan||Tōji (disambiguation){{!}}Tōji}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tō-ji
東寺 | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Toji 2015.JPG | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_upright = | alt = | caption = View of the Tō-ji complex | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 1 Kujōchō, [[Minami-ku, Kyoto|Minami-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = | religious_affiliation = Tōji-[[Shingon]] | deity = [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai]] (Bhaiṣajyaguru) | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = http://www.toji.or.jp/ | founded_by = [[Emperor Kanmu]] |established=796| year_completed = 1644 (Reconstruction) }} {{Nihongo|'''Tō-ji Temple'''|東寺|Tō-ji|"East Temple"}}, also known as {{Nihongo|'''Kyō-ō-gokoku-ji'''|教王護国寺|''The Temple for the Defense of the Nation by Means of the King of Doctrines''}} is a [[Shingon]] [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] temple in the [[Minami-ku, Kyoto|Minami-ku]] ward of [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. Founded in 796, it was one of the only three Buddhist temples allowed in the city at the time it became the capital of Japan. As such it has a long history, housing treasures and documents from the early [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]] and the [[Tang dynasty]], and with buildings in its complex covering the [[Kamakura period|Kamakura]], [[Muromachi period|Muromachi]], [[Momoyama period|Momoyama]], and [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]s. Five of these buildings have been designated [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in two different categories: the Lotus Flower Gate (''rengemon''), the Miei Hall (''mieidō''), the Golden Hall (''kondō'') and the five-storied Pagoda (''gojūnotō'') ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|temple buildings]]) and the Kanchiin Guest Hall (''kanchiin kyakuden'') ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)|residences]]). Tō-ji was designated a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] in 1994, as part of the [[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]. ==History== Tō-ji was founded in the early [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 111. The temple dates from 796, two years after the capital moved to [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]]. Together with its partner Sai-ji, and the temple [[Shingon-in]] (located in the [[:en:Heian Palace|Heian Palace]]), it was one of only three Buddhist temples allowed in the capital at the time and is the only of the three to survive to the present. It once had a partner, [[Sai-ji]] (West Temple) and, together, they stood alongside the [[Rajōmon|Rashomon]], the gate to the Heian capital. It was formerly known as {{Nihongo|'''Kyō-ō-gokoku-ji'''|教王護国寺|''The Temple for the Defense of the Nation by Means of the King of Doctrines''}}{{cite book | last = Hakeda | first = Yoshito S. | title = Kūkai and His Major Works | publisher = Columbia University Press | year = 1972 | isbn = 0-231-05933-7 | page = 7}} which indicates that it previously functioned as a temple providing protection for the nation. Tō-ji is located in [[Minami-ku, Kyoto|Minami-ku]] near the intersection of [[:en:Ōmiya Street|Ōmiya Street]] and Kujō Street, southwest of [[Kyōto Station]]. Tō-ji is often associated with Kōbō Daishi ([[Kūkai]]). Though Tō-ji began to decline at the end of the Heian period, it came back into the spotlight with the rise of Daishi Shinko (worshipping of Kōbō Daishi) in the Kamakura period. The well-known Buddhist priest was put in charge of Tō-ji in 823 by order of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]]. The temple's principal image is of [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi]] Nyorai, the Medicine Buddha. Many religious services for Daishi are held in {{Nihongo|Miei-dō|御影堂}} (or {{Nihongo|Daishi-dō|大師堂}}, in another name), the residence of Kōbō Daishi.{{cite web |url=http://www.toji.or.jp/mieidou.shtml |title=東寺 - 御影堂 〈大師堂〉 / Mieido House (Daishido House) |website=www.toji.or.jp |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090619211021/http://www.toji.or.jp/mieidou.shtml |archive-date=2009-06-19}} In 1586, the temple was seriously damaged by an [[:en:1586 Tenshō earthquake|earthquake]].{{cite book |last1=Utsu |first1=Tokuji |last2=Shima |first2=Etsuzo |last3=Yoshii |first3=Toshiyoshi |last4=Yamashina |first4=Kenichiro |title=地震の事典 |date=2001 |publisher=Asakura Shoten}} ==Architecture== [[File:Toji_-_Five-storied_Pagoda.JPG|thumb|left|''Five-storied Pagoda'' (National Treasure)]] [[File:Kyoto To-ji Pagode 17.jpg|thumb|right|Close-up of the ''Five-storied Pagoda'']] [[File:Five roofs of Toji-ji, Kyoto.jpg|thumb|Five roofs of Toji-ji, Kyoto]] ===Five-storey Pagoda=== The {{nihongo|five-storied Pagoda|五重塔|gojūnotō}} of Tō-ji dates from 1643 ([[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]), when it was rebuilt by order of the third [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] Shōgun, [[Tokugawa Iemitsu|Iemitsu]]. The original pagoda was built in the 9th century, but it was destroyed and rebuilt four times before reaching its current state. It was designated a [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure]] in 1952.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1834 |title=教王護国寺五重塔 |author= |publisher=Cultural Heritage Online |access-date=2019-06-21 |language=ja}} The pagoda stands {{convert|54.8|m|ft|abbr=off|sp=us}} high, and is the tallest [[wood]]en tower in Japan. Entrance into the pagoda itself is permitted only on special occasions, but it is usually open and the interior can be seen from the outside. It houses relics, sculptures, and paintings. At ground level, there are statues of four Buddhas facing different directions.{{cite web |url=https://toji.or.jp/en/inside/index.html |title=Inside of To-ji |author= |publisher=To-ji |access-date=2019-06-21 }} ===Kondo=== The ''Kondo'' or Golden Hall is the main hall of the temple and contains a statue of Yakushi from 1603. ===Miedo=== The Miedo is dedicated to Kobo Daishi, also called Kukai, the temple's founder. It stands on the location of his original residence. The hall is opened on the 21st of each month when a memorial service is held for Kukai. ===Temple grounds=== The grounds feature a garden and pond, in which turtles and koi swim. The grounds also house an academically rigorous private school, Rakunan, from which many students are sent to elite universities. ===Architectural Analysis=== Tō-ji was rebuilt in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], verging on the [[Kamakura Period]].{{Cite web|url=https://www.britannica.com/art/Japanese-architecture|title=Japanese architecture | History, Characteristics, & Facts|website=Encyclopedia Britannica}} During this rebuild, Tō-ji was dedicated to be a Shingon [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] ([[Shingon]], a form of [[Vajrayana Buddhism]], was brought to [[Japan]] by [[Kūkai]], a priest in 806).{{cite book | last = Ryuichi | first = Abe | title = The Weaving of Mantra: Kukai and the Construction of Esoteric Buddhist Discourse | publisher = Columbia University Press | year = 1999 | isbn = 0-231-11286-6 }} These temples were typically built in the mountains and utilized more natural and demographic design elements, dictating the resulting architectural layout. In the Kamakura period, Japanese architects began to utilize technology to resist damage from earthquakes, rainfall, sun, and heat damage. These fortifications were integrated into the remodeling of Tō-ji. This style of building defending against the natural elements evolved into the [[Zenshūyō]] style, seen later on in the Kamakura period. This style utilizes the "hidden roof" innovation.{{Cite web|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/|title=:: JAANUS :: Terminology of Japanese Architecture & Art History|website=www.aisf.or.jp}} Zenshūyō style temples, such as Tō-ji, are characterized by linear spacing outlines of the [[:en:Garan|Garan]], hinging panel doors, cusped windows called [[Katōmado]], and decorative pent roofs called [[Mokoshi]]. Although containing many of the elements of Zenshūyō style architecture, the Tō-ji temple uses the natural land around it to dictate the layout of the garan, which is a technique used in the [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]] and Edo Period of [[Japanese Architecture]]. This correlates with the Shingon attribution by [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] in 823. The decorative mokoshi and outfitting of modern structural technology (of the time), were most likely integrated during the remodeling of the tower in the Kamakura Period. Recognizing the historical and spiritual significance of Tō-ji, [[United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|UNESCO]] designated it, along with several other treasures in [[Kyoto Prefecture]], as part of the "[[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto]]" [[World Heritage Site]]. ==Flea market== On the 21st of each month, a famous [[:en:Flea market|Flea market]] is held on the grounds of Tō-ji. This market is popularly called Kōbō-san, in honor of Kōbō Daishi ([[Kūkai]]), who died on the 21 of the third Month of 835 AD (22 April in the Western Calendar). The flea market features a variety of antiques, art, clothes, pottery, some food, and typical second-hand flea market goods. By far the largest market is held on December 21, as it is the last of the year. A similar market is held on the 25th of every month at [[Kitano Tenmangū]], also called Tenjin. A Kyoto proverb proclaims, "Fair weather at Tō-ji market means rainy weather at Tenjin market", calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. A smaller, less-crowded, antique-oriented market is held at the Tō-ji grounds on the first Sunday of each month. ==Sai-ji== {{close paraphrasing|date=April 2021}} The [[Rajōmon|Rashomon]] was formerly situated to the west of Tō-ji, though now only a marker remains, reachable a short walk west along Kujō street. A little further west was [[Sai-ji]] (West Temple), though now only a small park remains. Tō-ji and Sai-ji were built at the southern edge of the capital and were the only Buddhist temples officially allowed in Heian-kyō at the time. Sai-ji disappeared in the 16th century. The reason was bad irrigation of [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]] and the lack of funds to maintain it. A legend says that at the time of a great drought, Kūkai, the priest at Tō-ji, and Shubin, his colleague at Sai-ji, were both praying for rainfall. Kūkai succeeded where Shubin had failed, and Shubin, envious, shot an arrow at Kūkai. At that time a Jizō{{clarify|date=July 2016}} appeared and took the arrow instead of Kūkai, saving his life. The Jizō is near the ruins of [[Rajōmon|Rashōmon]]. It has been chipped where the arrow is thought to have hit. ==Events== In 1995, the temple was the stage of the [[Yanni]] World Tour, On July 7, 2007, one of the [[Live Earth concert, Kyoto|Live Earth concerts]] (held to raise awareness of the Earth's climate) was staged at Tō-ji; artists who played included [[:en:Bonnie Pink|Bonnie Pink]], [[Michael Nyman]], [[Rip Slyme]], [[UA (singer)|UA]] and the [[Yellow Magic Orchestra]]. ==Gallery== File:Kondo,_Toji_a087.jpg|Kondo File:Kyoto_Toji_Mieido_C0973.jpg|Miedo File:Kioto-Japon105.jpg|Yakushi File:Senju_Kannon,_Toji_a085.jpg|Senju Kannon File:Lecture Hall Toji Five Great Bosatsu.JPG|Dainiti and Amida File:Gozanze Myoo Toji Kodo.jpg|Gozanze Myoo File:Taishakuten Śakra, Tō-ji.jpg|Taishakuten File:Kongokai.jpg|Kongokai [[Mandala]] File:Taizokai.jpg|Taizokai [[Mandala]] File:Dai Itoku Myoo Yamantaka Toji.JPG|Dai Itoku Myoo File:Kyowogokokuji Monastery Tobatsu Bishamonten (280).jpg|Bishamonten File:Toji Temple, Kyoto, Japan 1.jpg File:Toji Temple, Kyoto, Japan 2.jpg ==See also== * [[List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] *[[List of tallest structures built before the 20th century]] *[[Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Kyoto]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category|Toji}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869''. Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Buddhism topics}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{coord|34|58|50|N|135|44|52|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} ]] ]] ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:8th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Pagodas in Japan]] [[Category:Kūkai]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:Tōji Shingon temples]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:796 establishments]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in the 790s]] }} [[sn:Tō-ji]] [[en:Tō-ji]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ca:Tō-ji]] [[cs:Tódži]] [[de:Tō-ji]] [[el:Τό-τζι]] [[es:Tō-ji]] [[eu:Tō-ji]] [[fa:تو-جی]] [[fi:Tō-ji]] [[fr:Tō-ji]] [[gan:東寺]] [[id:Tō-ji]] [[it:Tō-ji]] [[ja:東寺]] [[ko:도지]] [[lt:Todži]] [[ms:Tō-ji]] [[my:တိုးဂျိ]] [[nl:To-ji]] [[pl:Tō-ji]] [[pt:Kyo-o-gokoku-ji]] [[ru:То-дзи]] [[sh:Tō-ji]] [[sl:To-dži]] [[th:โทจิ]] [[tr:Tō-ji]] [[vi:Tō-ji]] [[wuu:东寺（京都）]] [[zh:東寺]] [[zh-yue:東寺]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

This style of building defending against the natural elements evolved into the [[Zenshūyō]] style, seen later on in the Kamakura period. This style utilizes the "hidden roof" innovation.{{Cite web|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/|title=:: JAANUS :: Terminology of Japanese Architecture & Art History|website=www.aisf.or.jp}} Zenshūyō style temples, such as Tō-ji, are characterized by linear spacing outlines of the [[:en:Garan|Garan]], hinging panel doors, cusped windows called [[Katōmado]], and decorative pent roofs called [[Mokoshi]]. Although containing many of the elements of Zenshūyō style architecture, the Tō-ji temple uses the natural land around it to dictate the layout of the garan, which is a technique used in the [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]] and Edo Period of [[Japanese Architecture]]. This correlates with the Shingon attribution by [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] in 823. The decorative mokoshi and outfitting of modern structural technology (of the time), were most likely integrated during the remodeling of the tower in the Kamakura Period. Recognizing the historical and spiritual significance of Tō-ji, [[United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|UNESCO]] designated it, along with several other treasures in [[Kyoto Prefecture]], as part of the "[[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]]" [[World Heritage Site]]. ==Flea market== On the 21st of each month, a famous [[:en:Flea market|Flea market]] is held on the grounds of Tō-ji. This market is popularly called Kōbō-san, in honor of Kōbō Daishi ([[Kūkai]]), who died on the 21 of the third Month of 835 AD (22 April in the Western Calendar). The flea market features a variety of antiques, art, clothes, pottery, some food, and typical second-hand flea market goods. By far the largest market is held on December 21, as it is the last of the year. A similar market is held on the 25th of every month at [[Kitano Tenmangū]], also called Tenjin. A Kyoto proverb proclaims, "Fair weather at Tō-ji market means rainy weather at Tenjin market", calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. A smaller, less-crowded, antique-oriented market is held at the Tō-ji grounds on the first Sunday of each month. ==Sai-ji== {{close paraphrasing|date=April 2021}} The [[Rajōmon|Rashomon]] was formerly situated to the west of Tō-ji, though now only a marker remains, reachable a short walk west along Kujō street. A little further west was [[Sai-ji]] (West Temple), though now only a small park remains. Tō-ji and Sai-ji were built at the southern edge of the capital and were the only Buddhist temples officially allowed in Heian-kyō at the time. Sai-ji disappeared in the 16th century. The reason was bad irrigation of [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]] and the lack of funds to maintain it. A legend says that at the time of a great drought, Kūkai, the priest at Tō-ji, and Shubin, his colleague at Sai-ji, were both praying for rainfall. Kūkai succeeded where Shubin had failed, and Shubin, envious, shot an arrow at Kūkai. At that time a Jizō{{clarify|date=July 2016}} appeared and took the arrow instead of Kūkai, saving his life. The Jizō is near the ruins of [[Rajōmon|Rashōmon]]. It has been chipped where the arrow is thought to have hit. ==Events== In 1995, the temple was the stage of the [[Yanni]] World Tour, On July 7, 2007, one of the [[Live Earth concert, Kyoto|Live Earth concerts]] (held to raise awareness of the Earth's climate) was staged at Tō-ji; artists who played included [[:en:Bonnie Pink|Bonnie Pink]], [[Michael Nyman]], [[Rip Slyme]], [[UA (singer)|UA]] and the [[Yellow Magic Orchestra]]. ==Gallery== File:Kondo,_Toji_a087.jpg|Kondo File:Kyoto_Toji_Mieido_C0973.jpg|Miedo File:Kioto-Japon105.jpg|Yakushi File:Senju_Kannon,_Toji_a085.jpg|Senju Kannon File:Lecture Hall Toji Five Great Bosatsu.JPG|Dainiti and Amida File:Gozanze Myoo Toji Kodo.jpg|Gozanze Myoo File:Taishakuten Śakra, Tō-ji.jpg|Taishakuten File:Kongokai.jpg|Kongokai [[Mandala]] File:Taizokai.jpg|Taizokai [[Mandala]] File:Dai Itoku Myoo Yamantaka Toji.JPG|Dai Itoku Myoo File:Kyowogokokuji Monastery Tobatsu Bishamonten (280).jpg|Bishamonten File:Toji Temple, Kyoto, Japan 1.jpg File:Toji Temple, Kyoto, Japan 2.jpg ==See also== * [[List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] *[[List of tallest structures built before the 20th century]] *[[Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Kyoto]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category|Toji}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869''. Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Buddhism topics}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{coord|34|58|50|N|135|44|52|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} ]] ]] ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:8th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Pagodas in Japan]] [[Category:Kūkai]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:Tōji Shingon temples]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:796 establishments]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in the 790s]] }} [[sn:Tō-ji]] [[en:Tō-ji]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ca:Tō-ji]] [[cs:Tódži]] [[de:Tō-ji]] [[el:Τό-τζι]] [[es:Tō-ji]] [[eu:Tō-ji]] [[fa:تو-جی]] [[fi:Tō-ji]] [[fr:Tō-ji]] [[gan:東寺]] [[id:Tō-ji]] [[it:Tō-ji]] [[ja:東寺]] [[ko:도지]] [[lt:Todži]] [[ms:Tō-ji]] [[my:တိုးဂျိ]] [[nl:To-ji]] [[pl:Tō-ji]] [[pt:Kyo-o-gokoku-ji]] [[ru:То-дзи]] [[sh:Tō-ji]] [[sl:To-dži]] [[th:โทจิ]] [[tr:Tō-ji]] [[vi:Tō-ji]] [[wuu:东寺（京都）]] [[zh:東寺]] [[zh-yue:東寺]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

{{Short description|Shingon Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{About|a Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan||Tōji (disambiguation){{!}}Tōji}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tō-ji
東寺 | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Toji 2015.JPG | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_upright = | alt = | caption = View of the Tō-ji complex | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 1 Kujōchō, [[Minami-ku, Kyoto|Minami-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = | religious_affiliation = Tōji-[[Shingon]] | deity = [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai]] (Bhaiṣajyaguru) | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = http://www.toji.or.jp/ | founded_by = [[Emperor Kanmu]] |established=796| year_completed = 1644 (Reconstruction) }} {{Nihongo|'''Tō-ji Temple'''|東寺|Tō-ji|"East Temple"}}, also known as {{Nihongo|'''Kyō-ō-gokoku-ji'''|教王護国寺|''The Temple for the Defense of the Nation by Means of the King of Doctrines''}} is a [[Shingon]] [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] temple in the [[Minami-ku, Kyoto|Minami-ku]] ward of [[Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Founded in 796, it was one of the only three Buddhist temples allowed in the city at the time it became the capital of Japan. As such it has a long history, housing treasures and documents from the early [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]] and the [[Tang dynasty]], and with buildings in its complex covering the [[Kamakura period|Kamakura]], [[Muromachi period|Muromachi]], [[Momoyama period|Momoyama]], and [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]s. Five of these buildings have been designated [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in two different categories: the Lotus Flower Gate (''rengemon''), the Miei Hall (''mieidō''), the Golden Hall (''kondō'') and the five-storied Pagoda (''gojūnotō'') ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|temple buildings]]) and the Kanchiin Guest Hall (''kanchiin kyakuden'') ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)|residences]]). Tō-ji was designated a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] in 1994, as part of the [[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]]. ==History== Tō-ji was founded in the early [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 111. The temple dates from 796, two years after the capital moved to [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]]. Together with its partner Sai-ji, and the temple [[Shingon-in]] (located in the [[:en:Heian Palace|Heian Palace]]), it was one of only three Buddhist temples allowed in the capital at the time and is the only of the three to survive to the present. It once had a partner, [[Sai-ji]] (West Temple) and, together, they stood alongside the [[Rajōmon|Rashomon]], the gate to the Heian capital. It was formerly known as {{Nihongo|'''Kyō-ō-gokoku-ji'''|教王護国寺|''The Temple for the Defense of the Nation by Means of the King of Doctrines''}}{{cite book | last = Hakeda | first = Yoshito S. | title = Kūkai and His Major Works | publisher = Columbia University Press | year = 1972 | isbn = 0-231-05933-7 | page = 7}} which indicates that it previously functioned as a temple providing protection for the nation. Tō-ji is located in [[Minami-ku, Kyoto|Minami-ku]] near the intersection of [[:en:Ōmiya Street|Ōmiya Street]] and Kujō Street, southwest of [[Kyōto Station]]. Tō-ji is often associated with Kōbō Daishi ([[Kūkai]]). Though Tō-ji began to decline at the end of the Heian period, it came back into the spotlight with the rise of Daishi Shinko (worshipping of Kōbō Daishi) in the Kamakura period. The well-known Buddhist priest was put in charge of Tō-ji in 823 by order of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]]. The temple's principal image is of [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi]] Nyorai, the Medicine Buddha. Many religious services for Daishi are held in {{Nihongo|Miei-dō|御影堂}} (or {{Nihongo|Daishi-dō|大師堂}}, in another name), the residence of Kōbō Daishi.{{cite web |url=http://www.toji.or.jp/mieidou.shtml |title=東寺 - 御影堂 〈大師堂〉 / Mieido House (Daishido House) |website=www.toji.or.jp |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090619211021/http://www.toji.or.jp/mieidou.shtml |archive-date=2009-06-19}} In 1586, the temple was seriously damaged by an [[:en:1586 Tenshō earthquake|earthquake]].{{cite book |last1=Utsu |first1=Tokuji |last2=Shima |first2=Etsuzo |last3=Yoshii |first3=Toshiyoshi |last4=Yamashina |first4=Kenichiro |title=地震の事典 |date=2001 |publisher=Asakura Shoten}} ==Architecture== [[File:Toji_-_Five-storied_Pagoda.JPG|thumb|left|''Five-storied Pagoda'' (National Treasure)]] [[File:Kyoto To-ji Pagode 17.jpg|thumb|right|Close-up of the ''Five-storied Pagoda'']] [[File:Five roofs of Toji-ji, Kyoto.jpg|thumb|Five roofs of Toji-ji, Kyoto]] ===Five-storey Pagoda=== The {{nihongo|five-storied Pagoda|五重塔|gojūnotō}} of Tō-ji dates from 1643 ([[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]), when it was rebuilt by order of the third [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] Shōgun, [[Tokugawa Iemitsu|Iemitsu]]. The original pagoda was built in the 9th century, but it was destroyed and rebuilt four times before reaching its current state. It was designated a [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure]] in 1952.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1834 |title=教王護国寺五重塔 |author= |publisher=Cultural Heritage Online |access-date=2019-06-21 |language=ja}} The pagoda stands {{convert|54.8|m|ft|abbr=off|sp=us}} high, and is the tallest [[wood]]en tower in Japan. Entrance into the pagoda itself is permitted only on special occasions, but it is usually open and the interior can be seen from the outside. It houses relics, sculptures, and paintings. At ground level, there are statues of four Buddhas facing different directions.{{cite web |url=https://toji.or.jp/en/inside/index.html |title=Inside of To-ji |author= |publisher=To-ji |access-date=2019-06-21 }} ===Kondo=== The ''Kondo'' or Golden Hall is the main hall of the temple and contains a statue of Yakushi from 1603. ===Miedo=== The Miedo is dedicated to Kobo Daishi, also called Kukai, the temple's founder. It stands on the location of his original residence. The hall is opened on the 21st of each month when a memorial service is held for Kukai. ===Temple grounds=== The grounds feature a garden and pond, in which turtles and koi swim. The grounds also house an academically rigorous private school, Rakunan, from which many students are sent to elite universities. ===Architectural Analysis=== Tō-ji was rebuilt in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], verging on the [[Kamakura Period]].{{Cite web|url=https://www.britannica.com/art/Japanese-architecture|title=Japanese architecture | History, Characteristics, & Facts|website=Encyclopedia Britannica}} During this rebuild, Tō-ji was dedicated to be a Shingon [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] ([[Shingon]], a form of [[Vajrayana Buddhism]], was brought to [[:en:Japan|Japan]] by [[Kūkai]], a priest in 806).{{cite book | last = Ryuichi | first = Abe | title = The Weaving of Mantra: Kukai and the Construction of Esoteric Buddhist Discourse | publisher = Columbia University Press | year = 1999 | isbn = 0-231-11286-6 }} These temples were typically built in the mountains and utilized more natural and demographic design elements, dictating the resulting architectural layout. In the Kamakura period, Japanese architects began to utilize technology to resist damage from earthquakes, rainfall, sun, and heat damage. These fortifications were integrated into the remodeling of Tō-ji. This style of building defending against the natural elements evolved into the [[Zenshūyō]] style, seen later on in the Kamakura period. This style utilizes the "hidden roof" innovation.{{Cite web|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/|title=:: JAANUS :: Terminology of Japanese Architecture & Art History|website=www.aisf.or.jp}} Zenshūyō style temples, such as Tō-ji, are characterized by linear spacing outlines of the [[:en:Garan|Garan]], hinging panel doors, cusped windows called [[Katōmado]], and decorative pent roofs called [[Mokoshi]]. Although containing many of the elements of Zenshūyō style architecture, the Tō-ji temple uses the natural land around it to dictate the layout of the garan, which is a technique used in the [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]] and Edo Period of [[Japanese Architecture]]. This correlates with the Shingon attribution by [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] in 823. The decorative mokoshi and outfitting of modern structural technology (of the time), were most likely integrated during the remodeling of the tower in the Kamakura Period. Recognizing the historical and spiritual significance of Tō-ji, [[United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|UNESCO]] designated it, along with several other treasures in [[Kyoto Prefecture]], as part of the "[[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]]" [[World Heritage Site]]. ==Flea market== On the 21st of each month, a famous [[:en:Flea market|Flea market]] is held on the grounds of Tō-ji. This market is popularly called Kōbō-san, in honor of Kōbō Daishi ([[Kūkai]]), who died on the 21 of the third Month of 835 AD (22 April in the Western Calendar). The flea market features a variety of antiques, art, clothes, pottery, some food, and typical second-hand flea market goods. By far the largest market is held on December 21, as it is the last of the year. A similar market is held on the 25th of every month at [[Kitano Tenmangū]], also called Tenjin. A Kyoto proverb proclaims, "Fair weather at Tō-ji market means rainy weather at Tenjin market", calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. A smaller, less-crowded, antique-oriented market is held at the Tō-ji grounds on the first Sunday of each month. ==Sai-ji== {{close paraphrasing|date=April 2021}} The [[Rajōmon|Rashomon]] was formerly situated to the west of Tō-ji, though now only a marker remains, reachable a short walk west along Kujō street. A little further west was [[Sai-ji]] (West Temple), though now only a small park remains. Tō-ji and Sai-ji were built at the southern edge of the capital and were the only Buddhist temples officially allowed in Heian-kyō at the time. Sai-ji disappeared in the 16th century. The reason was bad irrigation of [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]] and the lack of funds to maintain it. A legend says that at the time of a great drought, Kūkai, the priest at Tō-ji, and Shubin, his colleague at Sai-ji, were both praying for rainfall. Kūkai succeeded where Shubin had failed, and Shubin, envious, shot an arrow at Kūkai. At that time a Jizō{{clarify|date=July 2016}} appeared and took the arrow instead of Kūkai, saving his life. The Jizō is near the ruins of [[Rajōmon|Rashōmon]]. It has been chipped where the arrow is thought to have hit. ==Events== In 1995, the temple was the stage of the [[Yanni]] World Tour, On July 7, 2007, one of the [[Live Earth concert, Kyoto|Live Earth concerts]] (held to raise awareness of the Earth's climate) was staged at Tō-ji; artists who played included [[:en:Bonnie Pink|Bonnie Pink]], [[Michael Nyman]], [[Rip Slyme]], [[UA (singer)|UA]] and the [[Yellow Magic Orchestra]]. ==Gallery== File:Kondo,_Toji_a087.jpg|Kondo File:Kyoto_Toji_Mieido_C0973.jpg|Miedo File:Kioto-Japon105.jpg|Yakushi File:Senju_Kannon,_Toji_a085.jpg|Senju Kannon File:Lecture Hall Toji Five Great Bosatsu.JPG|Dainiti and Amida File:Gozanze Myoo Toji Kodo.jpg|Gozanze Myoo File:Taishakuten Śakra, Tō-ji.jpg|Taishakuten File:Kongokai.jpg|Kongokai [[Mandala]] File:Taizokai.jpg|Taizokai [[Mandala]] File:Dai Itoku Myoo Yamantaka Toji.JPG|Dai Itoku Myoo File:Kyowogokokuji Monastery Tobatsu Bishamonten (280).jpg|Bishamonten File:Toji Temple, Kyoto, Japan 1.jpg File:Toji Temple, Kyoto, Japan 2.jpg ==See also== * [[List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] *[[List of tallest structures built before the 20th century]] *[[Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Kyoto]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category|Toji}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869''. Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Buddhism topics}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{coord|34|58|50|N|135|44|52|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} ]] ]] ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:8th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Pagodas in Japan]] [[Category:Kūkai]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:Tōji Shingon temples]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:796 establishments]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in the 790s]] }} [[sn:Tō-ji]] [[en:Tō-ji]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ca:Tō-ji]] [[cs:Tódži]] [[de:Tō-ji]] [[el:Τό-τζι]] [[es:Tō-ji]] [[eu:Tō-ji]] [[fa:تو-جی]] [[fi:Tō-ji]] [[fr:Tō-ji]] [[gan:東寺]] [[id:Tō-ji]] [[it:Tō-ji]] [[ja:東寺]] [[ko:도지]] [[lt:Todži]] [[ms:Tō-ji]] [[my:တိုးဂျိ]] [[nl:To-ji]] [[pl:Tō-ji]] [[pt:Kyo-o-gokoku-ji]] [[ru:То-дзи]] [[sh:Tō-ji]] [[sl:To-dži]] [[th:โทจิ]] [[tr:Tō-ji]] [[vi:Tō-ji]] [[wuu:东寺（京都）]] [[zh:東寺]] [[zh-yue:東寺]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

The well-known Buddhist priest was put in charge of Tō-ji in 823 by order of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]]. The temple's principal image is of [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi]] Nyorai, the Medicine Buddha. Many religious services for Daishi are held in {{Nihongo|Miei-dō|御影堂}} (or {{Nihongo|Daishi-dō|大師堂}}, in another name), the residence of Kōbō Daishi.{{cite web |url=http://www.toji.or.jp/mieidou.shtml |title=東寺 - 御影堂 〈大師堂〉 / Mieido House (Daishido House) |website=www.toji.or.jp |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090619211021/http://www.toji.or.jp/mieidou.shtml |archive-date=2009-06-19}} In 1586, the temple was seriously damaged by an [[:en:1586 Tenshō earthquake|earthquake]].{{cite book |last1=Utsu |first1=Tokuji |last2=Shima |first2=Etsuzo |last3=Yoshii |first3=Toshiyoshi |last4=Yamashina |first4=Kenichiro |title=地震の事典 |date=2001 |publisher=Asakura Shoten}} ==Architecture== [[File:Toji_-_Five-storied_Pagoda.JPG|thumb|left|''Five-storied Pagoda'' (National Treasure)]] [[File:Kyoto To-ji Pagode 17.jpg|thumb|right|Close-up of the ''Five-storied Pagoda'']] [[File:Five roofs of Toji-ji, Kyoto.jpg|thumb|Five roofs of Toji-ji, Kyoto]] ===Five-storey Pagoda=== The {{nihongo|five-storied Pagoda|五重塔|gojūnotō}} of Tō-ji dates from 1643 ([[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]), when it was rebuilt by order of the third [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] Shōgun, [[Tokugawa Iemitsu|Iemitsu]]. The original pagoda was built in the 9th century, but it was destroyed and rebuilt four times before reaching its current state. It was designated a [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure]] in 1952.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1834 |title=教王護国寺五重塔 |author= |publisher=Cultural Heritage Online |access-date=2019-06-21 |language=ja}} The pagoda stands {{convert|54.8|m|ft|abbr=off|sp=us}} high, and is the tallest [[wood]]en tower in Japan. Entrance into the pagoda itself is permitted only on special occasions, but it is usually open and the interior can be seen from the outside. It houses relics, sculptures, and paintings. At ground level, there are statues of four Buddhas facing different directions.{{cite web |url=https://toji.or.jp/en/inside/index.html |title=Inside of To-ji |author= |publisher=To-ji |access-date=2019-06-21 }} ===Kondo=== The ''Kondo'' or Golden Hall is the main hall of the temple and contains a statue of Yakushi from 1603. ===Miedo=== The Miedo is dedicated to Kobo Daishi, also called Kukai, the temple's founder. It stands on the location of his original residence. The hall is opened on the 21st of each month when a memorial service is held for Kukai. ===Temple grounds=== The grounds feature a garden and pond, in which turtles and koi swim. The grounds also house an academically rigorous private school, Rakunan, from which many students are sent to elite universities. ===Architectural Analysis=== Tō-ji was rebuilt in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], verging on the [[Kamakura Period]].{{Cite web|url=https://www.britannica.com/art/Japanese-architecture|title=Japanese architecture | History, Characteristics, & Facts|website=Encyclopedia Britannica}} During this rebuild, Tō-ji was dedicated to be a Shingon [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] ([[Shingon]], a form of [[Vajrayana Buddhism]], was brought to [[:en:Japan|Japan]] by [[Kūkai]], a priest in 806).{{cite book | last = Ryuichi | first = Abe | title = The Weaving of Mantra: Kukai and the Construction of Esoteric Buddhist Discourse | publisher = Columbia University Press | year = 1999 | isbn = 0-231-11286-6 }} These temples were typically built in the mountains and utilized more natural and demographic design elements, dictating the resulting architectural layout. In the Kamakura period, Japanese architects began to utilize technology to resist damage from earthquakes, rainfall, sun, and heat damage. These fortifications were integrated into the remodeling of Tō-ji. This style of building defending against the natural elements evolved into the [[Zenshūyō]] style, seen later on in the Kamakura period. This style utilizes the "hidden roof" innovation.{{Cite web|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/|title=:: JAANUS :: Terminology of Japanese Architecture & Art History|website=www.aisf.or.jp}} Zenshūyō style temples, such as Tō-ji, are characterized by linear spacing outlines of the [[:en:Garan|Garan]], hinging panel doors, cusped windows called [[Katōmado]], and decorative pent roofs called [[Mokoshi]]. Although containing many of the elements of Zenshūyō style architecture, the Tō-ji temple uses the natural land around it to dictate the layout of the garan, which is a technique used in the [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]] and Edo Period of [[:en:Japanese Architecture|Japanese Architecture]]. This correlates with the Shingon attribution by [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] in 823. The decorative mokoshi and outfitting of modern structural technology (of the time), were most likely integrated during the remodeling of the tower in the Kamakura Period. Recognizing the historical and spiritual significance of Tō-ji, [[United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|UNESCO]] designated it, along with several other treasures in [[Kyoto Prefecture]], as part of the "[[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]]" [[World Heritage Site]]. ==Flea market== On the 21st of each month, a famous [[:en:Flea market|Flea market]] is held on the grounds of Tō-ji. This market is popularly called Kōbō-san, in honor of Kōbō Daishi ([[Kūkai]]), who died on the 21 of the third Month of 835 AD (22 April in the Western Calendar). The flea market features a variety of antiques, art, clothes, pottery, some food, and typical second-hand flea market goods. By far the largest market is held on December 21, as it is the last of the year. A similar market is held on the 25th of every month at [[Kitano Tenmangū]], also called Tenjin. A Kyoto proverb proclaims, "Fair weather at Tō-ji market means rainy weather at Tenjin market", calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. A smaller, less-crowded, antique-oriented market is held at the Tō-ji grounds on the first Sunday of each month. ==Sai-ji== {{close paraphrasing|date=April 2021}} The [[Rajōmon|Rashomon]] was formerly situated to the west of Tō-ji, though now only a marker remains, reachable a short walk west along Kujō street. A little further west was [[Sai-ji]] (West Temple), though now only a small park remains. Tō-ji and Sai-ji were built at the southern edge of the capital and were the only Buddhist temples officially allowed in Heian-kyō at the time. Sai-ji disappeared in the 16th century. The reason was bad irrigation of [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]] and the lack of funds to maintain it. A legend says that at the time of a great drought, Kūkai, the priest at Tō-ji, and Shubin, his colleague at Sai-ji, were both praying for rainfall. Kūkai succeeded where Shubin had failed, and Shubin, envious, shot an arrow at Kūkai. At that time a Jizō{{clarify|date=July 2016}} appeared and took the arrow instead of Kūkai, saving his life. The Jizō is near the ruins of [[Rajōmon|Rashōmon]]. It has been chipped where the arrow is thought to have hit. ==Events== In 1995, the temple was the stage of the [[Yanni]] World Tour, On July 7, 2007, one of the [[Live Earth concert, Kyoto|Live Earth concerts]] (held to raise awareness of the Earth's climate) was staged at Tō-ji; artists who played included [[:en:Bonnie Pink|Bonnie Pink]], [[Michael Nyman]], [[Rip Slyme]], [[UA (singer)|UA]] and the [[Yellow Magic Orchestra]]. ==Gallery== File:Kondo,_Toji_a087.jpg|Kondo File:Kyoto_Toji_Mieido_C0973.jpg|Miedo File:Kioto-Japon105.jpg|Yakushi File:Senju_Kannon,_Toji_a085.jpg|Senju Kannon File:Lecture Hall Toji Five Great Bosatsu.JPG|Dainiti and Amida File:Gozanze Myoo Toji Kodo.jpg|Gozanze Myoo File:Taishakuten Śakra, Tō-ji.jpg|Taishakuten File:Kongokai.jpg|Kongokai [[Mandala]] File:Taizokai.jpg|Taizokai [[Mandala]] File:Dai Itoku Myoo Yamantaka Toji.JPG|Dai Itoku Myoo File:Kyowogokokuji Monastery Tobatsu Bishamonten (280).jpg|Bishamonten File:Toji Temple, Kyoto, Japan 1.jpg File:Toji Temple, Kyoto, Japan 2.jpg ==See also== * [[List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] *[[List of tallest structures built before the 20th century]] *[[Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Kyoto]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category|Toji}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869''. Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Buddhism topics}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{coord|34|58|50|N|135|44|52|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} ]] ]] ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:8th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Pagodas in Japan]] [[Category:Kūkai]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:Tōji Shingon temples]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:796 establishments]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in the 790s]] }} [[sn:Tō-ji]] [[en:Tō-ji]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ca:Tō-ji]] [[cs:Tódži]] [[de:Tō-ji]] [[el:Τό-τζι]] [[es:Tō-ji]] [[eu:Tō-ji]] [[fa:تو-جی]] [[fi:Tō-ji]] [[fr:Tō-ji]] [[gan:東寺]] [[id:Tō-ji]] [[it:Tō-ji]] [[ja:東寺]] [[ko:도지]] [[lt:Todži]] [[ms:Tō-ji]] [[my:တိုးဂျိ]] [[nl:To-ji]] [[pl:Tō-ji]] [[pt:Kyo-o-gokoku-ji]] [[ru:То-дзи]] [[sh:Tō-ji]] [[sl:To-dži]] [[th:โทจิ]] [[tr:Tō-ji]] [[vi:Tō-ji]] [[wuu:东寺（京都）]] [[zh:東寺]] [[zh-yue:東寺]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

{{Short description|Shingon Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{About|a Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan||Tōji (disambiguation){{!}}Tōji}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tō-ji
東寺 | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Toji 2015.JPG | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_upright = | alt = | caption = View of the Tō-ji complex | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 1 Kujōchō, [[Minami-ku, Kyoto|Minami-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = | religious_affiliation = Tōji-[[Shingon]] | deity = [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai]] (Bhaiṣajyaguru) | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = http://www.toji.or.jp/ | founded_by = [[Emperor Kanmu]] |established=796| year_completed = 1644 (Reconstruction) }} {{Nihongo|'''Tō-ji Temple'''|東寺|Tō-ji|"East Temple"}}, also known as {{Nihongo|'''Kyō-ō-gokoku-ji'''|教王護国寺|''The Temple for the Defense of the Nation by Means of the King of Doctrines''}} is a [[Shingon]] [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] temple in the [[Minami-ku, Kyoto|Minami-ku]] ward of [[Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Founded in 796, it was one of the only three Buddhist temples allowed in the city at the time it became the capital of Japan. As such it has a long history, housing treasures and documents from the early [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]] and the [[Tang dynasty]], and with buildings in its complex covering the [[:en:Kamakura Period|Kamakura]], [[Muromachi period|Muromachi]], [[Momoyama period|Momoyama]], and [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]s. Five of these buildings have been designated [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in two different categories: the Lotus Flower Gate (''rengemon''), the Miei Hall (''mieidō''), the Golden Hall (''kondō'') and the five-storied Pagoda (''gojūnotō'') ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|temple buildings]]) and the Kanchiin Guest Hall (''kanchiin kyakuden'') ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)|residences]]). Tō-ji was designated a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] in 1994, as part of the [[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]]. ==History== Tō-ji was founded in the early [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 111. The temple dates from 796, two years after the capital moved to [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]]. Together with its partner Sai-ji, and the temple [[Shingon-in]] (located in the [[:en:Heian Palace|Heian Palace]]), it was one of only three Buddhist temples allowed in the capital at the time and is the only of the three to survive to the present. It once had a partner, [[Sai-ji]] (West Temple) and, together, they stood alongside the [[Rajōmon|Rashomon]], the gate to the Heian capital. It was formerly known as {{Nihongo|'''Kyō-ō-gokoku-ji'''|教王護国寺|''The Temple for the Defense of the Nation by Means of the King of Doctrines''}}{{cite book | last = Hakeda | first = Yoshito S. | title = Kūkai and His Major Works | publisher = Columbia University Press | year = 1972 | isbn = 0-231-05933-7 | page = 7}} which indicates that it previously functioned as a temple providing protection for the nation. Tō-ji is located in [[Minami-ku, Kyoto|Minami-ku]] near the intersection of [[:en:Ōmiya Street|Ōmiya Street]] and Kujō Street, southwest of [[Kyōto Station]]. Tō-ji is often associated with Kōbō Daishi ([[Kūkai]]). Though Tō-ji began to decline at the end of the Heian period, it came back into the spotlight with the rise of Daishi Shinko (worshipping of Kōbō Daishi) in the Kamakura period. The well-known Buddhist priest was put in charge of Tō-ji in 823 by order of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]]. The temple's principal image is of [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi]] Nyorai, the Medicine Buddha. Many religious services for Daishi are held in {{Nihongo|Miei-dō|御影堂}} (or {{Nihongo|Daishi-dō|大師堂}}, in another name), the residence of Kōbō Daishi.{{cite web |url=http://www.toji.or.jp/mieidou.shtml |title=東寺 - 御影堂 〈大師堂〉 / Mieido House (Daishido House) |website=www.toji.or.jp |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090619211021/http://www.toji.or.jp/mieidou.shtml |archive-date=2009-06-19}} In 1586, the temple was seriously damaged by an [[:en:1586 Tenshō earthquake|earthquake]].{{cite book |last1=Utsu |first1=Tokuji |last2=Shima |first2=Etsuzo |last3=Yoshii |first3=Toshiyoshi |last4=Yamashina |first4=Kenichiro |title=地震の事典 |date=2001 |publisher=Asakura Shoten}} ==Architecture== [[File:Toji_-_Five-storied_Pagoda.JPG|thumb|left|''Five-storied Pagoda'' (National Treasure)]] [[File:Kyoto To-ji Pagode 17.jpg|thumb|right|Close-up of the ''Five-storied Pagoda'']] [[File:Five roofs of Toji-ji, Kyoto.jpg|thumb|Five roofs of Toji-ji, Kyoto]] ===Five-storey Pagoda=== The {{nihongo|five-storied Pagoda|五重塔|gojūnotō}} of Tō-ji dates from 1643 ([[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]), when it was rebuilt by order of the third [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] Shōgun, [[Tokugawa Iemitsu|Iemitsu]]. The original pagoda was built in the 9th century, but it was destroyed and rebuilt four times before reaching its current state. It was designated a [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure]] in 1952.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1834 |title=教王護国寺五重塔 |author= |publisher=Cultural Heritage Online |access-date=2019-06-21 |language=ja}} The pagoda stands {{convert|54.8|m|ft|abbr=off|sp=us}} high, and is the tallest [[wood]]en tower in Japan. Entrance into the pagoda itself is permitted only on special occasions, but it is usually open and the interior can be seen from the outside. It houses relics, sculptures, and paintings. At ground level, there are statues of four Buddhas facing different directions.{{cite web |url=https://toji.or.jp/en/inside/index.html |title=Inside of To-ji |author= |publisher=To-ji |access-date=2019-06-21 }} ===Kondo=== The ''Kondo'' or Golden Hall is the main hall of the temple and contains a statue of Yakushi from 1603. ===Miedo=== The Miedo is dedicated to Kobo Daishi, also called Kukai, the temple's founder. It stands on the location of his original residence. The hall is opened on the 21st of each month when a memorial service is held for Kukai. ===Temple grounds=== The grounds feature a garden and pond, in which turtles and koi swim. The grounds also house an academically rigorous private school, Rakunan, from which many students are sent to elite universities. ===Architectural Analysis=== Tō-ji was rebuilt in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], verging on the [[:en:Kamakura Period|Kamakura Period]].{{Cite web|url=https://www.britannica.com/art/Japanese-architecture|title=Japanese architecture | History, Characteristics, & Facts|website=Encyclopedia Britannica}} During this rebuild, Tō-ji was dedicated to be a Shingon [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] ([[Shingon]], a form of [[Vajrayana Buddhism]], was brought to [[:en:Japan|Japan]] by [[Kūkai]], a priest in 806).{{cite book | last = Ryuichi | first = Abe | title = The Weaving of Mantra: Kukai and the Construction of Esoteric Buddhist Discourse | publisher = Columbia University Press | year = 1999 | isbn = 0-231-11286-6 }} These temples were typically built in the mountains and utilized more natural and demographic design elements, dictating the resulting architectural layout. In the Kamakura period, Japanese architects began to utilize technology to resist damage from earthquakes, rainfall, sun, and heat damage. These fortifications were integrated into the remodeling of Tō-ji. This style of building defending against the natural elements evolved into the [[Zenshūyō]] style, seen later on in the Kamakura period. This style utilizes the "hidden roof" innovation.{{Cite web|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/|title=:: JAANUS :: Terminology of Japanese Architecture & Art History|website=www.aisf.or.jp}} Zenshūyō style temples, such as Tō-ji, are characterized by linear spacing outlines of the [[:en:Garan|Garan]], hinging panel doors, cusped windows called [[Katōmado]], and decorative pent roofs called [[Mokoshi]]. Although containing many of the elements of Zenshūyō style architecture, the Tō-ji temple uses the natural land around it to dictate the layout of the garan, which is a technique used in the [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]] and Edo Period of [[:en:Japanese Architecture|Japanese Architecture]]. This correlates with the Shingon attribution by [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] in 823. The decorative mokoshi and outfitting of modern structural technology (of the time), were most likely integrated during the remodeling of the tower in the Kamakura Period. Recognizing the historical and spiritual significance of Tō-ji, [[United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|UNESCO]] designated it, along with several other treasures in [[Kyoto Prefecture]], as part of the "[[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]]" [[World Heritage Site]]. ==Flea market== On the 21st of each month, a famous [[:en:Flea market|Flea market]] is held on the grounds of Tō-ji. This market is popularly called Kōbō-san, in honor of Kōbō Daishi ([[Kūkai]]), who died on the 21 of the third Month of 835 AD (22 April in the Western Calendar). The flea market features a variety of antiques, art, clothes, pottery, some food, and typical second-hand flea market goods. By far the largest market is held on December 21, as it is the last of the year. A similar market is held on the 25th of every month at [[Kitano Tenmangū]], also called Tenjin. A Kyoto proverb proclaims, "Fair weather at Tō-ji market means rainy weather at Tenjin market", calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. A smaller, less-crowded, antique-oriented market is held at the Tō-ji grounds on the first Sunday of each month. ==Sai-ji== {{close paraphrasing|date=April 2021}} The [[Rajōmon|Rashomon]] was formerly situated to the west of Tō-ji, though now only a marker remains, reachable a short walk west along Kujō street. A little further west was [[Sai-ji]] (West Temple), though now only a small park remains. Tō-ji and Sai-ji were built at the southern edge of the capital and were the only Buddhist temples officially allowed in Heian-kyō at the time. Sai-ji disappeared in the 16th century. The reason was bad irrigation of [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]] and the lack of funds to maintain it. A legend says that at the time of a great drought, Kūkai, the priest at Tō-ji, and Shubin, his colleague at Sai-ji, were both praying for rainfall. Kūkai succeeded where Shubin had failed, and Shubin, envious, shot an arrow at Kūkai. At that time a Jizō{{clarify|date=July 2016}} appeared and took the arrow instead of Kūkai, saving his life. The Jizō is near the ruins of [[Rajōmon|Rashōmon]]. It has been chipped where the arrow is thought to have hit. ==Events== In 1995, the temple was the stage of the [[Yanni]] World Tour, On July 7, 2007, one of the [[Live Earth concert, Kyoto|Live Earth concerts]] (held to raise awareness of the Earth's climate) was staged at Tō-ji; artists who played included [[:en:Bonnie Pink|Bonnie Pink]], [[Michael Nyman]], [[Rip Slyme]], [[UA (singer)|UA]] and the [[Yellow Magic Orchestra]]. ==Gallery== File:Kondo,_Toji_a087.jpg|Kondo File:Kyoto_Toji_Mieido_C0973.jpg|Miedo File:Kioto-Japon105.jpg|Yakushi File:Senju_Kannon,_Toji_a085.jpg|Senju Kannon File:Lecture Hall Toji Five Great Bosatsu.JPG|Dainiti and Amida File:Gozanze Myoo Toji Kodo.jpg|Gozanze Myoo File:Taishakuten Śakra, Tō-ji.jpg|Taishakuten File:Kongokai.jpg|Kongokai [[Mandala]] File:Taizokai.jpg|Taizokai [[Mandala]] File:Dai Itoku Myoo Yamantaka Toji.JPG|Dai Itoku Myoo File:Kyowogokokuji Monastery Tobatsu Bishamonten (280).jpg|Bishamonten File:Toji Temple, Kyoto, Japan 1.jpg File:Toji Temple, Kyoto, Japan 2.jpg ==See also== * [[List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] *[[List of tallest structures built before the 20th century]] *[[Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Kyoto]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category|Toji}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Buddhism topics}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{coord|34|58|50|N|135|44|52|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} ]] ]] ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:8th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Pagodas in Japan]] [[Category:Kūkai]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:Tōji Shingon temples]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:796 establishments]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in the 790s]] }} [[sn:Tō-ji]] [[en:Tō-ji]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ca:Tō-ji]] [[cs:Tódži]] [[de:Tō-ji]] [[el:Τό-τζι]] [[es:Tō-ji]] [[eu:Tō-ji]] [[fa:تو-جی]] [[fi:Tō-ji]] [[fr:Tō-ji]] [[gan:東寺]] [[id:Tō-ji]] [[it:Tō-ji]] [[ja:東寺]] [[ko:도지]] [[lt:Todži]] [[ms:Tō-ji]] [[my:တိုးဂျိ]] [[nl:To-ji]] [[pl:Tō-ji]] [[pt:Kyo-o-gokoku-ji]] [[ru:То-дзи]] [[sh:Tō-ji]] [[sl:To-dži]] [[th:โทจิ]] [[tr:Tō-ji]] [[vi:Tō-ji]] [[wuu:东寺（京都）]] [[zh:東寺]] [[zh-yue:東寺]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

{{Short description|Shingon Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{About|a Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan||Tōji (disambiguation){{!}}Tōji}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tō-ji
東寺 | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Toji 2015.JPG | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_upright = | alt = | caption = View of the Tō-ji complex | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 1 Kujōchō, [[Minami-ku, Kyoto|Minami-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = | religious_affiliation = Tōji-[[Shingon]] | deity = [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai]] (Bhaiṣajyaguru) | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = http://www.toji.or.jp/ | founded_by = [[Emperor Kanmu]] |established=796| year_completed = 1644 (Reconstruction) }} {{Nihongo|'''Tō-ji Temple'''|東寺|Tō-ji|"East Temple"}}, also known as {{Nihongo|'''Kyō-ō-gokoku-ji'''|教王護国寺|''The Temple for the Defense of the Nation by Means of the King of Doctrines''}} is a [[Shingon]] [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] temple in the [[Minami-ku, Kyoto|Minami-ku]] ward of [[Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Founded in 796, it was one of the only three Buddhist temples allowed in the city at the time it became the capital of Japan. As such it has a long history, housing treasures and documents from the early [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]] and the [[Tang dynasty]], and with buildings in its complex covering the [[:en:Kamakura Period|Kamakura]], [[Muromachi period|Muromachi]], [[Momoyama period|Momoyama]], and [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]s. Five of these buildings have been designated [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasures]] in two different categories: the Lotus Flower Gate (''rengemon''), the Miei Hall (''mieidō''), the Golden Hall (''kondō'') and the five-storied Pagoda (''gojūnotō'') ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|temple buildings]]) and the Kanchiin Guest Hall (''kanchiin kyakuden'') ([[List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)|residences]]). Tō-ji was designated a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] in 1994, as part of the [[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]]. ==History== Tō-ji was founded in the early [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869'', p. 111. The temple dates from 796, two years after the capital moved to [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]]. Together with its partner Sai-ji, and the temple [[Shingon-in]] (located in the [[:en:Heian Palace|Heian Palace]]), it was one of only three Buddhist temples allowed in the capital at the time and is the only of the three to survive to the present. It once had a partner, [[Sai-ji]] (West Temple) and, together, they stood alongside the [[Rajōmon|Rashomon]], the gate to the Heian capital. It was formerly known as {{Nihongo|'''Kyō-ō-gokoku-ji'''|教王護国寺|''The Temple for the Defense of the Nation by Means of the King of Doctrines''}}{{cite book | last = Hakeda | first = Yoshito S. | title = Kūkai and His Major Works | publisher = Columbia University Press | year = 1972 | isbn = 0-231-05933-7 | page = 7}} which indicates that it previously functioned as a temple providing protection for the nation. Tō-ji is located in [[Minami-ku, Kyoto|Minami-ku]] near the intersection of [[:en:Ōmiya Street|Ōmiya Street]] and Kujō Street, southwest of [[Kyōto Station]]. Tō-ji is often associated with Kōbō Daishi ([[Kūkai]]). Though Tō-ji began to decline at the end of the Heian period, it came back into the spotlight with the rise of Daishi Shinko (worshipping of Kōbō Daishi) in the Kamakura period. The well-known Buddhist priest was put in charge of Tō-ji in 823 by order of [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]]. The temple's principal image is of [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi]] Nyorai, the Medicine Buddha. Many religious services for Daishi are held in {{Nihongo|Miei-dō|御影堂}} (or {{Nihongo|Daishi-dō|大師堂}}, in another name), the residence of Kōbō Daishi.{{cite web |url=http://www.toji.or.jp/mieidou.shtml |title=東寺 - 御影堂 〈大師堂〉 / Mieido House (Daishido House) |website=www.toji.or.jp |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090619211021/http://www.toji.or.jp/mieidou.shtml |archive-date=2009-06-19}} In 1586, the temple was seriously damaged by an [[:en:1586 Tenshō earthquake|earthquake]].{{cite book |last1=Utsu |first1=Tokuji |last2=Shima |first2=Etsuzo |last3=Yoshii |first3=Toshiyoshi |last4=Yamashina |first4=Kenichiro |title=地震の事典 |date=2001 |publisher=Asakura Shoten}} ==Architecture== [[File:Toji_-_Five-storied_Pagoda.JPG|thumb|left|''Five-storied Pagoda'' (National Treasure)]] [[File:Kyoto To-ji Pagode 17.jpg|thumb|right|Close-up of the ''Five-storied Pagoda'']] [[File:Five roofs of Toji-ji, Kyoto.jpg|thumb|Five roofs of Toji-ji, Kyoto]] ===Five-storey Pagoda=== The {{nihongo|five-storied Pagoda|五重塔|gojūnotō}} of Tō-ji dates from 1643 ([[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]]), when it was rebuilt by order of the third [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa]] Shōgun, [[Tokugawa Iemitsu|Iemitsu]]. The original pagoda was built in the 9th century, but it was destroyed and rebuilt four times before reaching its current state. It was designated a [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure]] in 1952.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/1834 |title=教王護国寺五重塔 |author= |publisher=Cultural Heritage Online |access-date=2019-06-21 |language=ja}} The pagoda stands {{convert|54.8|m|ft|abbr=off|sp=us}} high, and is the tallest [[wood]]en tower in Japan. Entrance into the pagoda itself is permitted only on special occasions, but it is usually open and the interior can be seen from the outside. It houses relics, sculptures, and paintings. At ground level, there are statues of four Buddhas facing different directions.{{cite web |url=https://toji.or.jp/en/inside/index.html |title=Inside of To-ji |author= |publisher=To-ji |access-date=2019-06-21 }} ===Kondo=== The ''Kondo'' or Golden Hall is the main hall of the temple and contains a statue of Yakushi from 1603. ===Miedo=== The Miedo is dedicated to Kobo Daishi, also called Kukai, the temple's founder. It stands on the location of his original residence. The hall is opened on the 21st of each month when a memorial service is held for Kukai. ===Temple grounds=== The grounds feature a garden and pond, in which turtles and koi swim. The grounds also house an academically rigorous private school, Rakunan, from which many students are sent to elite universities. ===Architectural Analysis=== Tō-ji was rebuilt in the early [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], verging on the [[:en:Kamakura Period|Kamakura Period]].{{Cite web|url=https://www.britannica.com/art/Japanese-architecture|title=Japanese architecture | History, Characteristics, & Facts|website=Encyclopedia Britannica}} During this rebuild, Tō-ji was dedicated to be a Shingon [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] ([[Shingon]], a form of [[Vajrayana Buddhism]], was brought to [[:en:Japan|Japan]] by [[Kūkai]], a priest in 806).{{cite book | last = Ryuichi | first = Abe | title = The Weaving of Mantra: Kukai and the Construction of Esoteric Buddhist Discourse | publisher = Columbia University Press | year = 1999 | isbn = 0-231-11286-6 }} These temples were typically built in the mountains and utilized more natural and demographic design elements, dictating the resulting architectural layout. In the Kamakura period, Japanese architects began to utilize technology to resist damage from earthquakes, rainfall, sun, and heat damage. These fortifications were integrated into the remodeling of Tō-ji. This style of building defending against the natural elements evolved into the [[Zenshūyō]] style, seen later on in the Kamakura period. This style utilizes the "hidden roof" innovation.{{Cite web|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/|title=:: JAANUS :: Terminology of Japanese Architecture & Art History|website=www.aisf.or.jp}} Zenshūyō style temples, such as Tō-ji, are characterized by linear spacing outlines of the [[:en:Garan|Garan]], hinging panel doors, cusped windows called [[Katōmado]], and decorative pent roofs called [[Mokoshi]]. Although containing many of the elements of Zenshūyō style architecture, the Tō-ji temple uses the natural land around it to dictate the layout of the garan, which is a technique used in the [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]] and Edo Period of [[:en:Japanese Architecture|Japanese Architecture]]. This correlates with the Shingon attribution by [[:en:Emperor Saga|Emperor Saga]] in 823. The decorative mokoshi and outfitting of modern structural technology (of the time), were most likely integrated during the remodeling of the tower in the Kamakura Period. Recognizing the historical and spiritual significance of Tō-ji, [[United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization|UNESCO]] designated it, along with several other treasures in [[Kyoto Prefecture]], as part of the "[[:en:Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)|Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)]]" [[World Heritage Site]]. ==Flea market== On the 21st of each month, a famous [[:en:Flea market|Flea market]] is held on the grounds of Tō-ji. This market is popularly called Kōbō-san, in honor of Kōbō Daishi ([[Kūkai]]), who died on the 21 of the third Month of 835 AD (22 April in the Western Calendar). The flea market features a variety of antiques, art, clothes, pottery, some food, and typical second-hand flea market goods. By far the largest market is held on December 21, as it is the last of the year. A similar market is held on the 25th of every month at [[Kitano Tenmangū]], also called Tenjin. A Kyoto proverb proclaims, "Fair weather at Tō-ji market means rainy weather at Tenjin market", calling to mind Kyoto's fickle weather. A smaller, less-crowded, antique-oriented market is held at the Tō-ji grounds on the first Sunday of each month. ==Sai-ji== {{close paraphrasing|date=April 2021}} The [[Rajōmon|Rashomon]] was formerly situated to the west of Tō-ji, though now only a marker remains, reachable a short walk west along Kujō street. A little further west was [[Sai-ji]] (West Temple), though now only a small park remains. Tō-ji and Sai-ji were built at the southern edge of the capital and were the only Buddhist temples officially allowed in Heian-kyō at the time. Sai-ji disappeared in the 16th century. The reason was bad irrigation of [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]] and the lack of funds to maintain it. A legend says that at the time of a great drought, Kūkai, the priest at Tō-ji, and Shubin, his colleague at Sai-ji, were both praying for rainfall. Kūkai succeeded where Shubin had failed, and Shubin, envious, shot an arrow at Kūkai. At that time a Jizō{{clarify|date=July 2016}} appeared and took the arrow instead of Kūkai, saving his life. The Jizō is near the ruins of [[Rajōmon|Rashōmon]]. It has been chipped where the arrow is thought to have hit. ==Events== In 1995, the temple was the stage of the [[Yanni]] World Tour, On July 7, 2007, one of the [[Live Earth concert, Kyoto|Live Earth concerts]] (held to raise awareness of the Earth's climate) was staged at Tō-ji; artists who played included [[:en:Bonnie Pink|Bonnie Pink]], [[Michael Nyman]], [[Rip Slyme]], [[UA (singer)|UA]] and the [[Yellow Magic Orchestra]]. ==Gallery== File:Kondo,_Toji_a087.jpg|Kondo File:Kyoto_Toji_Mieido_C0973.jpg|Miedo File:Kioto-Japon105.jpg|Yakushi File:Senju_Kannon,_Toji_a085.jpg|Senju Kannon File:Lecture Hall Toji Five Great Bosatsu.JPG|Dainiti and Amida File:Gozanze Myoo Toji Kodo.jpg|Gozanze Myoo File:Taishakuten Śakra, Tō-ji.jpg|Taishakuten File:Kongokai.jpg|Kongokai [[Mandala]] File:Taizokai.jpg|Taizokai [[Mandala]] File:Dai Itoku Myoo Yamantaka Toji.JPG|Dai Itoku Myoo File:Kyowogokokuji Monastery Tobatsu Bishamonten (280).jpg|Bishamonten File:Toji Temple, Kyoto, Japan 1.jpg File:Toji Temple, Kyoto, Japan 2.jpg ==See also== * [[List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] *[[:en:List of tallest structures built before the 20th century|List of tallest structures built before the 20th century]] *[[Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Kyoto]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category|Toji}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869''. Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Buddhism topics}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{coord|34|58|50|N|135|44|52|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} ]] ]] ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:8th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Pagodas in Japan]] [[Category:Kūkai]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:Tōji Shingon temples]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:796 establishments]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in the 790s]] }} [[sn:Tō-ji]] [[en:Tō-ji]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ca:Tō-ji]] [[cs:Tódži]] [[de:Tō-ji]] [[el:Τό-τζι]] [[es:Tō-ji]] [[eu:Tō-ji]] [[fa:تو-جی]] [[fi:Tō-ji]] [[fr:Tō-ji]] [[gan:東寺]] [[id:Tō-ji]] [[it:Tō-ji]] [[ja:東寺]] [[ko:도지]] [[lt:Todži]] [[ms:Tō-ji]] [[my:တိုးဂျိ]] [[nl:To-ji]] [[pl:Tō-ji]] [[pt:Kyo-o-gokoku-ji]] [[ru:То-дзи]] [[sh:Tō-ji]] [[sl:To-dži]] [[th:โทจิ]] [[tr:Tō-ji]] [[vi:Tō-ji]] [[wuu:东寺（京都）]] [[zh:東寺]] [[zh-yue:東寺]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

Kūkai succeeded where Shubin had failed, and Shubin, envious, shot an arrow at Kūkai. At that time a Jizō{{clarify|date=July 2016}} appeared and took the arrow instead of Kūkai, saving his life. The Jizō is near the ruins of [[Rajōmon|Rashōmon]]. It has been chipped where the arrow is thought to have hit. ==Events== In 1995, the temple was the stage of the [[Yanni]] World Tour, On July 7, 2007, one of the [[Live Earth concert, Kyoto|Live Earth concerts]] (held to raise awareness of the Earth's climate) was staged at Tō-ji; artists who played included [[:en:Bonnie Pink|Bonnie Pink]], [[Michael Nyman]], [[Rip Slyme]], [[UA (singer)|UA]] and the [[Yellow Magic Orchestra]]. ==Gallery== File:Kondo,_Toji_a087.jpg|Kondo File:Kyoto_Toji_Mieido_C0973.jpg|Miedo File:Kioto-Japon105.jpg|Yakushi File:Senju_Kannon,_Toji_a085.jpg|Senju Kannon File:Lecture Hall Toji Five Great Bosatsu.JPG|Dainiti and Amida File:Gozanze Myoo Toji Kodo.jpg|Gozanze Myoo File:Taishakuten Śakra, Tō-ji.jpg|Taishakuten File:Kongokai.jpg|Kongokai [[Mandala]] File:Taizokai.jpg|Taizokai [[Mandala]] File:Dai Itoku Myoo Yamantaka Toji.JPG|Dai Itoku Myoo File:Kyowogokokuji Monastery Tobatsu Bishamonten (280).jpg|Bishamonten File:Toji Temple, Kyoto, Japan 1.jpg File:Toji Temple, Kyoto, Japan 2.jpg ==See also== * [[List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (residences)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] *[[:en:List of tallest structures built before the 20th century|List of tallest structures built before the 20th century]] *[[Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Kyoto]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category|Toji}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794–1869''. Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{World Heritage Kyoto}} {{Buddhism topics}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{coord|34|58|50|N|135|44|52|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} ]] ]] ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:8th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Pagodas in Japan]] [[Category:Kūkai]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Shingon Buddhism]] [[Category:Tōji Shingon temples]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:796 establishments]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in the 790s]] }} [[sn:Tō-ji]] [[en:Tō-ji]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ca:Tō-ji]] [[cs:Tódži]] [[de:Tō-ji]] [[el:Τό-τζι]] [[es:Tō-ji]] [[eu:Tō-ji]] [[fa:تو-جی]] [[fi:Tō-ji]] [[fr:Tō-ji]] [[gan:東寺]] [[id:Tō-ji]] [[it:Tō-ji]] [[ja:東寺]] [[ko:도지]] [[lt:Todži]] [[ms:Tō-ji]] [[my:တိုးဂျိ]] [[nl:To-ji]] [[pl:Tō-ji]] [[pt:Kyo-o-gokoku-ji]] [[ru:То-дзи]] [[sh:Tō-ji]] [[sl:To-dži]] [[th:โทจิ]] [[tr:Tō-ji]] [[vi:Tō-ji]] [[wuu:东寺（京都）]] [[zh:東寺]] [[zh-yue:東寺]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]
